lesson_title: Writing a Model Card and Communicating Model Limitations

junior.question: What is a Model Card and why is it important in open-source AI development?
junior.core_intent: Assesses knowledge of model documentation standards.
junior.preparation_guide: Explain target users, evaluation results, training setups, and known limitations.
junior.explanation: A Model Card is a structured markdown document that details model architecture, intended use cases, training parameters, evaluation benchmarks, and safety limitations, ensuring transparency in AI deployments.
junior.star_framework_tip: Cite the core sections of a standard Model Card.
common_pitfalls: Treating Model Cards as marketing materials rather than technical documentation.

references.title: Model Cards for Model Reporting (Mitchell et al.)

mid_level.question: How do you calculate and document the carbon footprint of training an SLM in a Model Card?
mid_level.explanation: Compute emissions using: $Emissions (kg CO2e) = Power (kW) \times Hours \times PUE \times CarbonIntensity$. Document this value under the environmental impact section of the Model Card to promote carbon accountability.
mid_level.star_framework_tip: Write out the carbon emission calculation formula.
common_pitfalls: Only reporting GPU power, ignoring CPU memory, cooling, and grid efficiency metrics.

senior.question: Design an automated CI/CD pipeline step that generates a machine-readable Model Card using evaluation outputs.
senior.preparation_guide: Parse test reports, format values into Markdown templates, and commit the updated Model Card to git.
senior.explanation: Use a script triggered after training that parses evaluation logs (accuracy, latency, bias metrics) and injects them into a pre-defined YAML/Markdown template, committing the updated Model Card automatically.
senior.star_framework_tip: Detail the integration with GitHub Actions or Jenkins.
common_pitfalls: Hardcoding metric values in template files instead of parsing logs programmatically.
